annotations.label: Simply typed lambda calculus (STLC)

anchor.snippet: is a typed interpretation of the lambda calculus with only one type constructor

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of lambda-calculus term/type syntax at all.

annotations.label: Simple type (extensions: products, coproducts, System T, PCF)

anchor.snippet: The term simple type is also used to refer to extensions of the simply typed lambda calculus

annotations.note: No Mathlib formalization of System T, PCF, or extended simple-type systems.

annotations.label: Church's base types (o and ι)

anchor.snippet: Church used only two base types

annotations.note: Church's historical base-type system for simple type theory is not represented in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Function type

anchor.snippet: refers to the type of functions that, given an input of type

annotations.note: Function types are Lean's own meta-level arrow types, not a Mathlib-formalized object-language construct of STLC.

annotations.label: Syntax of types (grammar)

anchor.snippet: To define the types, a set of base types

annotations.note: No inductive type-grammar for STLC types exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Example: infinite set of generated types

anchor.snippet: generates an infinite set of types starting with

annotations.note: Illustrative example of the (unformalized) STLC type grammar.

annotations.label: Term constants for base types

anchor.snippet: A set of term constants is also fixed for the base types.

annotations.note: No Mathlib notion of STLC term constants over base types.

annotations.label: Term syntax (BNF)

anchor.snippet: The syntax of the simply typed lambda calculus is essentially that of the lambda calculus itself.

annotations.note: Mathlib contains no inductive term datatype for the (typed) lambda calculus.

annotations.label: Bound variable and closed term

anchor.snippet: is bound if it is inside of an abstraction binding

annotations.note: No lambda-calculus term syntax in Mathlib to define bound/closed terms over.

annotations.label: Typing context and typing assumption

anchor.snippet: To define the set of well-typed lambda terms of a given type, one defines a typing relation between terms and types.

annotations.note: No STLC typing-context formalization exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Typing relation, judgment, and derivation

anchor.snippet: Instances of the typing relation are called typing judgments

annotations.note: No formal STLC typing-judgment relation is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Typing rules (Var, Const, Abs, App)

anchor.snippet: in the context, then

annotations.note: The Var/Const/Abs/App typing rules of STLC are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: I, K, S combinators as closed terms

anchor.snippet: Examples of closed terms, i.e. terms typable in the empty context, are

annotations.note: No SKI-combinator formalization tied to STLC typing exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Order of a type

anchor.snippet: the order of a type measures the depth of the most left-nested arrow

annotations.note: No notion of 'order of a type' for STLC types is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intrinsic semantics

anchor.snippet: An intrinsic semantics only assigns meaning to well-typed terms

annotations.note: No formalized intrinsic-semantics interpretation of STLC in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Example: identity term on integers vs Booleans

anchor.snippet: For example, the identity term

annotations.note: Illustrative example dependent on the unformalized STLC term/type system.

annotations.label: Extrinsic semantics

anchor.snippet: an extrinsic semantics assigns meaning to terms regardless of typing

annotations.note: No formalized extrinsic-semantics interpretation of untyped/typed lambda terms in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equational theory of STLC (βη-equivalence)

anchor.snippet: The simply typed lambda calculus (STLC) has the same equational theory of βη-equivalence as untyped lambda calculus

annotations.note: No βη-equivalence relation or comparison theorem for typed/untyped lambda calculus exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Beta reduction rule

anchor.snippet: The equation for beta reduction

annotations.note: No beta-reduction relation on lambda terms is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eta reduction rule

anchor.snippet: the equation for eta reduction

annotations.note: No eta-reduction relation on lambda terms is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type safety and termination of evaluation strategies

anchor.snippet: type safety is a fundamental property of all of these evaluation strategies

annotations.note: No operational semantics or type-safety theorem for STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambek's STLC–CCC correspondence

anchor.snippet: is the internal language of Cartesian closed categories

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes cartesian closed categories (CartesianMonoidalCategory + MonoidalClosed) abstractly but does not construct the syntactic STLC category or state Lambek's correspondence theorem.

annotations.label: Pairing, projection, and unit term rules

anchor.snippet: There are typing rules for pairing

annotations.note: No STLC term syntax exists in Mathlib to attach pairing/projection/unit typing rules to.

annotations.label: Unit term reduces to nil

anchor.snippet: This last is read as

annotations.note: No unit-term reduction rule for STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: STLC as a category (objects=types, morphisms=term classes)

anchor.snippet: The above can then be turned into a category by taking the types as the objects

annotations.note: Mathlib has the abstract target structure (cartesian/monoidal closed category) but does not build the specific syntactic category of STLC types and term-equivalence-classes.

annotations.label: Functorial extension to language homomorphisms

anchor.snippet: This correspondence can be extended to include

annotations.note: No notion of STLC language homomorphism or its functorial extension exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extension to closed symmetric monoidal categories (linear types)

anchor.snippet: Part of this correspondence can be extended to closed symmetric monoidal categories

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes (symmetric) monoidal closed categories abstractly, but the specific linear-type/STLC correspondence result is not stated.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard correspondence for STLC

anchor.snippet: via the Curry–Howard isomorphism

annotations.note: Curry–Howard underlies Lean's own type theory but is not stated as an explicit Mathlib theorem about STLC.

annotations.label: Hindley–Milner inference correctness

anchor.snippet: The inference algorithm is terminating, sound, and complete

annotations.note: No Hindley–Milner type-inference algorithm or its correctness proof exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bidirectional checking and synthesis judgments

anchor.snippet: Another alternative presentation of simply typed lambda calculus is based on bidirectional type checking

annotations.note: No bidirectional type-checking system for STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bidirectional typing rules [1]–[4]

anchor.snippet: Observe that rules [1]–[4] are nearly identical to rules (1)–(4) above

annotations.note: No bidirectional typing rules for STLC exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bidirectional coercion rules [5]–[6]

anchor.snippet: Finally, we explain rules [5] and [6] as follows

annotations.note: No bidirectional coercion rules for STLC exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Completeness of bidirectional system with minimal annotations

anchor.snippet: any well-typed but unannotated term can be checked in the bidirectional system

annotations.note: No formalization of bidirectional-system completeness for STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strong normalization of STLC

anchor.snippet: the simply typed lambda calculus is strongly normalizing

annotations.note: No strong-normalization theorem for STLC exists in Mathlib (no STLC reduction relation at all).

annotations.label: STLC is not Turing complete

anchor.snippet: the simply typed lambda calculus is not Turing complete

annotations.note: No Turing-completeness notion connected to STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tait's strong normalization theorem (1967)

annotations.note: Tait's proof method/theorem for STLC normalization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: No decidability result for STLC βη-equivalence exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statman's non-elementary complexity result (1979)

anchor.snippet: Statman showed in 1979 that the normalisation problem is non-elementary

annotations.note: No complexity-theoretic result about STLC normalization exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mairson's simplified proof

anchor.snippet: a proof that was later simplified by Mairson

annotations.note: No formalization of Mairson's proof exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: TOWER-completeness of the normalization problem

anchor.snippet: it is complete in the complexity class called TOWER

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complexity-class formalization such as TOWER, nor STLC normalization decision problem.

annotations.label: Berger–Schwichtenberg normalization by evaluation (1991)

anchor.snippet: A purely semantic normalisation proof

annotations.note: Normalization-by-evaluation for STLC is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: No lambda-term unification problem or undecidability result exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Huet's 3rd order unification undecidability (1973)

anchor.snippet: Huet showed in 1973 that 3rd order unification is undecidable

annotations.note: No formalization of Huet's higher-order unification undecidability result exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Goldfarb's 2nd order unification undecidability (1981)

anchor.snippet: by showing that 2nd order unification is already undecidable

annotations.note: No formalization of Goldfarb's second-order unification undecidability result exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stirling's decidability of higher-order matching (2006/2009)

anchor.snippet: A proof that higher order matching

annotations.note: No formalization of higher-order matching decidability exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Church numerals

anchor.snippet: We can encode natural numbers by terms of the type

annotations.note: Mathlib's Nat is a native inductive type, not the Church-numeral lambda-term encoding described here; no such encoding exists in Mathlib.

annotations.note: No formalization of Schwichtenberg's representable-functions characterization exists in Mathlib.

annotations.note: No full set-theoretic model construction for STLC exists in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Friedman showed in 1975 that this interpretation is complete for

annotations.note: No formalization of Statman's Typical Ambiguity Theorem exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite model property (corollary)

anchor.snippet: A corollary of this is that the finite model property holds

annotations.note: No finite-model-property result for STLC exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plotkin's logical relations (1973)

anchor.snippet: Plotkin introduced logical relations in 1973 to characterize the elements of a model that are definable by lambda terms

annotations.note: No logical-relations framework for STLC/model definability exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jung–Tiuryn characterization theorem (1993)

anchor.snippet: In 1993 Jung and Tiuryn showed that a general form of logical relation

annotations.note: No formalization of the Jung–Tiuryn characterization theorem exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plotkin–Statman conjecture

anchor.snippet: Plotkin and Statman conjectured that it is decidable whether a given element of a model generated from finite sets is definable by a lambda term

annotations.note: No formalization of the Plotkin–Statman conjecture exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Loader's refutation of the Plotkin–Statman conjecture (2001)

anchor.snippet: The conjecture was shown to be false by Loader in 2001

annotations.note: No formalization of Loader's refutation result exists in Mathlib.
